title: Respiratory Failure — Type 2
oneLiner: Hypercapnic (PaCO₂ >6.0 kPa) ± hypoxic respiratory failure — most often from COPD, obesity hypoventilation, or neuromuscular disease.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Severe respiratory acidosis (pH <7.25) with coma** — **Key finding:** GCS <12, rapid drop, pupillary signs? *Do not* wait for NIV — call ICU for intubation immediately.
- **Tension pneumothorax** — **Key finding:** tracheal deviation away, absent breath sounds, hyperresonance, +pulsus paradoxus. *Needle decompression before ABG.*
- **Upper airway obstruction (mucus plug / foreign body / bilateral vocal cord palsy)** — **Key finding:** no air entry despite respiratory effort, stridor or silence. *Immediate laryngoscopy or bronchoscopy.*
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms**
  - Breathlessness (worse on lying flat in neuromuscular/obesity)
  - Morning headache + confusional episodes
  - Daytime somnolence (CO₂ narcosis)
  - Peripheral oedema (cor pulmonale)
  - Opioid/sedative ingestion may present with drowsiness + ≤8 RR
- **Key exam findings**
  - Bounding pulse, warm peripheries (CO₂ vasodilation)
  - Asterixis (flap), papilloedema (chronic)
  - Pursed-lip breathing, accessory muscle use, paradoxical abdominal movement (neuromuscular)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **COPD exacerbation** → most common cause of acute T2RF in ward; history of smoking, barrel chest, wheeze
- **Acute severe asthma** → less common cause of hypercapnia; PEFR <33% predicted, silent chest
- **Opiate overdose** → pinpoint pupils, RR<8, improved after naloxone (may need repeat)
- **Neuromuscular / chest wall disorders** → rapid shallow breathing, weak cough, low VC, normal lung fields on CXR
- **Obesity hypoventilation syndrome (OHS)** → BMI >35, daytime hypercapnia, sleep apnoea symptoms, elevated HCO₃⁻
- **Central sleep apnoea / Cheyne-Stokes** → often in heart failure or neurological disease; history of paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea
- **Splitter:** “Does the patient wheeze?” → If yes: COPD/ asthma; if no + normal CXR + low VC → neuromuscular/OHS
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside**
  - **Arterial blood gas (ABG)** — *do not wait*; repeat after 30-60 min on NIV
  - SpO₂ (caution: may be falsely normal in T2RF due to O₂ therapy)
  - Peak expiratory flow rate (PEFR) if obstructive airway disease suspected — sick patients may not be able to perform
  - Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) — serial monitoring essential
- **Bloods**
  - FBC, U&E, CRP, blood cultures (if fever)
  - Troponin (if chest pain, especially in cor pulmonale)
  - Thyroid function (myxoedema can cause hypoventilation)
  - Drug screen (opiates, benzodiazepines) if overdose suspected
- **Imaging**
  - **CXR (AP erect/portable)** — exclude pneumothorax, consolidation, pulmonary oedema, chest wall deformity
  - **ECG** — right atrial enlargement (P pulmonale), right ventricular strain (S1Q3T3?), arrhythmias
- **Special tests**
  - **ABG** — diagnostic; also for calculation of A-a gradient (normal in pure T2RF, widened if accompanied by V/Q mismatch)
  - **Spirometry** (when stable) — FEV1/FVC ratio <0.7 for COPD
  - **Sleep study** — if OHS or nocturnal hypoventilation suspected
- **Diagnostic criteria**
  - T2RF: PaCO₂ >6.0 kPa (or >6.5 kPa if local threshold) ± PaO₂ <8.0 kPa
- **Never-miss test**
  - **ABG** — without it you *cannot* confirm T2RF, guide oxygen therapy, nor assess acidosis severity.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED)**
  - **Controlled oxygen** — target SpO₂ **88-92%** (O₂ alert card if known CO₂ retainer); use venturi mask 24-28% or nasal cannulae at 1-2 L/min
  - **ABG before and after oxygen change**
  - **NIV (BiPAP)** if pH <7.35 + PaCO₂ >6.0 + able to protect airway: settings: IPAP 10-15 cmH₂O, EPAP 4-6 cmH₂O, FiO₂ titrated; escalate to ICU if pH <7.25 or GCS<12
  - For suspected opiate overdose: naloxone 400 mcg IV/IM (repeat PRN); *[check local policy for repeat doses]*
  - For suspected pneumothorax: needle decompression (2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line)
- **Ward (first 24-72h)**
  - Continue NIV — aim for PaCO₂ trend downwards, pH >7.35; wean off as able.
  - Treat underlying cause:
    - COPD exacerbation → nebulised salbutamol 5mg QDS + ipratropium 0.5mg QDS, prednisolone 30-40mg PO OD (5-7 days), empirical antibiotics (amoxicillin 500mg TDS PO or doxycycline 200mg stat then 100mg OD)
    - Pneumonia → add antibiotics as per local guidelines (usually co-amoxiclav ± clarithromycin)
    - Heart failure in cor pulmonale → cautious diuresis (furosemide 40mg IV/PO) – avoid overdiuresis that drops preload
  - Daily blood gases (or VBG) – trend pH, PaCO₂
  - Chest physiotherapy + airway clearance (if neuromuscular)
  - Consider lung-protective ventilation if intubation required (low tidal volume)
- **Long-term / outpatient**
  - Home oxygen therapy assessment (6 weeks post-discharge: ABG off O₂, SpO₂ <92% → consider long-term oxygen)
  - Pulmonary rehabilitation (COPD)
  - Smoking cessation + inhaler optimisation
  - Nocturnal NIV (if persistent daytime hypercapnia, OHS, or neuromuscular disease)
  - Immunisations (influenza, pneumococcal)
- **Default Orders (if admitted)**
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride 1 L/24h (caution in cor pulmonale – avoid fluid overload)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) if no contraindication
  - **Diet:** NBM initially if on NIV (aspiration risk); once stable, normal diet with modified consistency if dysphagia
  - **Observations:** 4-hourly NEWS2 + continuous SpO₂ and respiratory rate while on NIV; consider telemetry if arrhythmia
sections.Drugs & Doses:
| Drug | Dose & Route | Duration | Notes |
|------|--------------|----------|-------|
| Salbutamol nebuliser | 5mg QDS via nebuliser | Acute (wean as able) | Use 2.5mg in elderly/frail; may need continuous if severe |
| Ipratropium bromide nebs | 0.5mg QDS via nebuliser | Acute | Add if not responding to salbutamol alone |
| Prednisolone | 30-40mg PO OD | 5-7 days | No need to taper in short courses |
| Amoxicillin | 500mg TDS PO | 5-7 days | [Check local policy] — alternative: doxycycline (200mg stat, then 100mg OD) |
| Furosemide (if cor pulmonale) | 40mg IV/PO BD | PRN or regular | Monitor renal function & weight daily |
| Naloxone (overdose) | 400 mcg IV/IM, repeat PRN | Short half-life (~1h) | Watch for re-narcosis; consider infusion (10 mcg/kg/h) |
- **Key contraindications / interactions**
  - Beta-blockers (non-selective) worsen bronchospasm
  - Opiates + benzodiazepines → respiratory depression in T2RF patient
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment**
  - Penicillins: reduce dose if CrCl <30 mL/min (e.g., amoxicillin 500mg BD)
  - Furosemide: reversible effect in hepatic impairment; monitor Na, K
- **Most dangerous dose error**
  - **Oxygen overcorrection:** targeting SpO₂ >92% in known CO₂ retainers → worsens hypercapnia, acidosis, and can precipitate narcosis. *Always prescribe O₂ target (88-92%) and use controlled delivery.*
sections.Complications:
- **Acute**
  - Respiratory arrest (if pH <7.15 or GCS <8)
  - Pneumothorax from NIV (especially with large PPEP)
  - Ventilator-associated pneumonia (if intubated)
  - Opiate-related ileus (from naloxone if overdosed)
  - Hypercapnic seizure (rare)
- **Chronic**
  - Cor pulmonale (right heart failure)
  - Secondary polycythaemia (from chronic hypoxia)
  - Pulmonary hypertension
  - Pressure sores (from NIV mask)
  - Dependence on home oxygen ± NIV
sections.Prognosis:
- Acute T2RF (COPD exacerbation): in-hospital mortality ~10-20% if NIV required; those who survive have reduced quality of life and high 1-year readmission rate (~30-40%).
- Chronic T2RF (OHS/neuromuscular): median survival 5-10 years depending on underlying disease, but long-term NIV improves survival and symptoms.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always get a pre- and post-NIV ABG** — it tells you if therapy is working (pH rising) or failing (pH falling, PaCO₂ rising). Escalate within 1-2h if failing.
- **Check drug chart for all sedatives** — even a single dose of morphine or zopiclone can precipitate acute-on-chronic hypercapnia.
- **Use a purpose-written O₂ prescription** — include target range (88-92%) and device (venturi mask 24%-28%); review daily.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Giving high-flow oxygen** (e.g., non-rebreather at 15L/min) to a patient with T2RF — this suppresses hypoxic drive and worsens hypercapnia. **Always start controlled O₂**.
- **Ignoring the ABG because SpO₂ looks good** — a patient on O₂ may have normal SpO₂ but dangerous PaCO₂ and acidosis.
- **Delaying NIV** in a patient with pH <7.25 while waiting for CXR or blood results — the intervention is time-critical. Call ICU if not confident.
- **Using loperamide or anti-motility agents** in diarrhoea associated with COPD exacerbation (not relevant here, but a common pitfall in hospital) — avoid.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening breathlessness despite usual inhalers
  - Confusion or drowsiness (early sign of CO₂ narcosis)
  - New or purulent sputum
  - Cyanosis or peripheral oedema (especially if known cor pulmonale)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Respiratory clinic within 4-6 weeks
  - Spirometry + functional assessment (if not already done)
  - Home oxygen assessment (if desaturation on exercise or during sleep)
  - Sleep clinic referral if OHS or nocturnal hypoventilation suspected
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially if mainly respiratory component)
  - pH <7.25 despite NIV (or falling pH)
  - Rising PaCO₂ while on NIV (by >0.5 kPa/h)
  - GCS ≤12 (or drop by ≥2)
  - Respiratory rate >35 or <8
  - Haemodynamic instability: systolic BP <90 mmHg, lactate >4 mmol/L
  - Intolerance of NIV (unable to fit mask, claustrophobia)
  - Confirmed pneumothorax requiring chest drain
- **For immediate intubation:** pH <7.15 + GCS <8 + inadequate ventilation after 15 min of NIV trial or airway compromise.

rawMarkdown:
## Respiratory Failure — Type 2
**One-liner:** Hypercapnic (PaCO₂ >6.0 kPa) ± hypoxic respiratory failure — most often from COPD, obesity hypoventilation, or neuromuscular disease.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Severe respiratory acidosis (pH <7.25) with coma** — **Key finding:** GCS <12, rapid drop, pupillary signs? *Do not* wait for NIV — call ICU for intubation immediately.
- **Tension pneumothorax** — **Key finding:** tracheal deviation away, absent breath sounds, hyperresonance, +pulsus paradoxus. *Needle decompression before ABG.*
- **Upper airway obstruction (mucus plug / foreign body / bilateral vocal cord palsy)** — **Key finding:** no air entry despite respiratory effort, stridor or silence. *Immediate laryngoscopy or bronchoscopy.*

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms**
  - Breathlessness (worse on lying flat in neuromuscular/obesity)
  - Morning headache + confusional episodes
  - Daytime somnolence (CO₂ narcosis)
  - Peripheral oedema (cor pulmonale)
  - Opioid/sedative ingestion may present with drowsiness + ≤8 RR
- **Key exam findings**
  - Bounding pulse, warm peripheries (CO₂ vasodilation)
  - Asterixis (flap), papilloedema (chronic)
  - Pursed-lip breathing, accessory muscle use, paradoxical abdominal movement (neuromuscular)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **COPD exacerbation** → most common cause of acute T2RF in ward; history of smoking, barrel chest, wheeze
- **Acute severe asthma** → less common cause of hypercapnia; PEFR <33% predicted, silent chest
- **Opiate overdose** → pinpoint pupils, RR<8, improved after naloxone (may need repeat)
- **Neuromuscular / chest wall disorders** → rapid shallow breathing, weak cough, low VC, normal lung fields on CXR
- **Obesity hypoventilation syndrome (OHS)** → BMI >35, daytime hypercapnia, sleep apnoea symptoms, elevated HCO₃⁻
- **Central sleep apnoea / Cheyne-Stokes** → often in heart failure or neurological disease; history of paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea
- **Splitter:** “Does the patient wheeze?” → If yes: COPD/ asthma; if no + normal CXR + low VC → neuromuscular/OHS

### Investigations
- **Bedside**
  - **Arterial blood gas (ABG)** — *do not wait*; repeat after 30-60 min on NIV
  - SpO₂ (caution: may be falsely normal in T2RF due to O₂ therapy)
  - Peak expiratory flow rate (PEFR) if obstructive airway disease suspected — sick patients may not be able to perform
  - Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) — serial monitoring essential
- **Bloods**
  - FBC, U&E, CRP, blood cultures (if fever)
  - Troponin (if chest pain, especially in cor pulmonale)
  - Thyroid function (myxoedema can cause hypoventilation)
  - Drug screen (opiates, benzodiazepines) if overdose suspected
- **Imaging**
  - **CXR (AP erect/portable)** — exclude pneumothorax, consolidation, pulmonary oedema, chest wall deformity
  - **ECG** — right atrial enlargement (P pulmonale), right ventricular strain (S1Q3T3?), arrhythmias
- **Special tests**
  - **ABG** — diagnostic; also for calculation of A-a gradient (normal in pure T2RF, widened if accompanied by V/Q mismatch)
  - **Spirometry** (when stable) — FEV1/FVC ratio <0.7 for COPD
  - **Sleep study** — if OHS or nocturnal hypoventilation suspected
- **Diagnostic criteria**
  - T2RF: PaCO₂ >6.0 kPa (or >6.5 kPa if local threshold) ± PaO₂ <8.0 kPa
- **Never-miss test**
  - **ABG** — without it you *cannot* confirm T2RF, guide oxygen therapy, nor assess acidosis severity.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED)**
  - **Controlled oxygen** — target SpO₂ **88-92%** (O₂ alert card if known CO₂ retainer); use venturi mask 24-28% or nasal cannulae at 1-2 L/min
  - **ABG before and after oxygen change**
  - **NIV (BiPAP)** if pH <7.35 + PaCO₂ >6.0 + able to protect airway: settings: IPAP 10-15 cmH₂O, EPAP 4-6 cmH₂O, FiO₂ titrated; escalate to ICU if pH <7.25 or GCS<12
  - For suspected opiate overdose: naloxone 400 mcg IV/IM (repeat PRN); *[check local policy for repeat doses]*
  - For suspected pneumothorax: needle decompression (2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line)
- **Ward (first 24-72h)**
  - Continue NIV — aim for PaCO₂ trend downwards, pH >7.35; wean off as able.
  - Treat underlying cause:
    - COPD exacerbation → nebulised salbutamol 5mg QDS + ipratropium 0.5mg QDS, prednisolone 30-40mg PO OD (5-7 days), empirical antibiotics (amoxicillin 500mg TDS PO or doxycycline 200mg stat then 100mg OD)
    - Pneumonia → add antibiotics as per local guidelines (usually co-amoxiclav ± clarithromycin)
    - Heart failure in cor pulmonale → cautious diuresis (furosemide 40mg IV/PO) – avoid overdiuresis that drops preload
  - Daily blood gases (or VBG) – trend pH, PaCO₂
  - Chest physiotherapy + airway clearance (if neuromuscular)
  - Consider lung-protective ventilation if intubation required (low tidal volume)
- **Long-term / outpatient**
  - Home oxygen therapy assessment (6 weeks post-discharge: ABG off O₂, SpO₂ <92% → consider long-term oxygen)
  - Pulmonary rehabilitation (COPD)
  - Smoking cessation + inhaler optimisation
  - Nocturnal NIV (if persistent daytime hypercapnia, OHS, or neuromuscular disease)
  - Immunisations (influenza, pneumococcal)
- **Default Orders (if admitted)**
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride 1 L/24h (caution in cor pulmonale – avoid fluid overload)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) if no contraindication
  - **Diet:** NBM initially if on NIV (aspiration risk); once stable, normal diet with modified consistency if dysphagia
  - **Observations:** 4-hourly NEWS2 + continuous SpO₂ and respiratory rate while on NIV; consider telemetry if arrhythmia

### Drugs & Doses
| Drug | Dose & Route | Duration | Notes |
|------|--------------|----------|-------|
| Salbutamol nebuliser | 5mg QDS via nebuliser | Acute (wean as able) | Use 2.5mg in elderly/frail; may need continuous if severe |
| Ipratropium bromide nebs | 0.5mg QDS via nebuliser | Acute | Add if not responding to salbutamol alone |
| Prednisolone | 30-40mg PO OD | 5-7 days | No need to taper in short courses |
| Amoxicillin | 500mg TDS PO | 5-7 days | [Check local policy] — alternative: doxycycline (200mg stat, then 100mg OD) |
| Furosemide (if cor pulmonale) | 40mg IV/PO BD | PRN or regular | Monitor renal function & weight daily |
| Naloxone (overdose) | 400 mcg IV/IM, repeat PRN | Short half-life (~1h) | Watch for re-narcosis; consider infusion (10 mcg/kg/h) |
- **Key contraindications / interactions**
  - Beta-blockers (non-selective) worsen bronchospasm
  - Opiates + benzodiazepines → respiratory depression in T2RF patient
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment**
  - Penicillins: reduce dose if CrCl <30 mL/min (e.g., amoxicillin 500mg BD)
  - Furosemide: reversible effect in hepatic impairment; monitor Na, K
- **Most dangerous dose error**
  - **Oxygen overcorrection:** targeting SpO₂ >92% in known CO₂ retainers → worsens hypercapnia, acidosis, and can precipitate narcosis. *Always prescribe O₂ target (88-92%) and use controlled delivery.*

### Complications
- **Acute**
  - Respiratory arrest (if pH <7.15 or GCS <8)
  - Pneumothorax from NIV (especially with large PPEP)
  - Ventilator-associated pneumonia (if intubated)
  - Opiate-related ileus (from naloxone if overdosed)
  - Hypercapnic seizure (rare)
- **Chronic**
  - Cor pulmonale (right heart failure)
  - Secondary polycythaemia (from chronic hypoxia)
  - Pulmonary hypertension
  - Pressure sores (from NIV mask)
  - Dependence on home oxygen ± NIV

### Prognosis
- Acute T2RF (COPD exacerbation): in-hospital mortality ~10-20% if NIV required; those who survive have reduced quality of life and high 1-year readmission rate (~30-40%).
- Chronic T2RF (OHS/neuromuscular): median survival 5-10 years depending on underlying disease, but long-term NIV improves survival and symptoms.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always get a pre- and post-NIV ABG** — it tells you if therapy is working (pH rising) or failing (pH falling, PaCO₂ rising). Escalate within 1-2h if failing.
- **Check drug chart for all sedatives** — even a single dose of morphine or zopiclone can precipitate acute-on-chronic hypercapnia.
- **Use a purpose-written O₂ prescription** — include target range (88-92%) and device (venturi mask 24%-28%); review daily.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Giving high-flow oxygen** (e.g., non-rebreather at 15L/min) to a patient with T2RF — this suppresses hypoxic drive and worsens hypercapnia. **Always start controlled O₂**.
- **Ignoring the ABG because SpO₂ looks good** — a patient on O₂ may have normal SpO₂ but dangerous PaCO₂ and acidosis.
- **Delaying NIV** in a patient with pH <7.25 while waiting for CXR or blood results — the intervention is time-critical. Call ICU if not confident.
- **Using loperamide or anti-motility agents** in diarrhoea associated with COPD exacerbation (not relevant here, but a common pitfall in hospital) — avoid.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening breathlessness despite usual inhalers
  - Confusion or drowsiness (early sign of CO₂ narcosis)
  - New or purulent sputum
  - Cyanosis or peripheral oedema (especially if known cor pulmonale)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Respiratory clinic within 4-6 weeks
  - Spirometry + functional assessment (if not already done)
  - Home oxygen assessment (if desaturation on exercise or during sleep)
  - Sleep clinic referral if OHS or nocturnal hypoventilation suspected

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially if mainly respiratory component)
  - pH <7.25 despite NIV (or falling pH)
  - Rising PaCO₂ while on NIV (by >0.5 kPa/h)
  - GCS ≤12 (or drop by ≥2)
  - Respiratory rate >35 or <8
  - Haemodynamic instability: systolic BP <90 mmHg, lactate >4 mmol/L
  - Intolerance of NIV (unable to fit mask, claustrophobia)
  - Confirmed pneumothorax requiring chest drain
- **For immediate intubation:** pH <7.15 + GCS <8 + inadequate ventilation after 15 min of NIV trial or airway compromise.
